frontmatter.title: Numerical Optimization Toolbox for Reduced OCPs

children.value: This lecture is a numerical optimization toolbox for the reduced formulation

children.html: min⁡u∈Rnf(u).\min_{u\in\mathbb{R}^n} f(u).u∈Rnmin​f(u).

children.value: The same algorithms will later be reused for PDE-constrained optimization, where

children.html: f(u)=J(S(u),u)f(u)=J(S(u),u)f(u)=J(S(u),u)

children.value: and each gradient evaluation typically requires state/adjoint solves.
isolate the role of Armijo/Wolfe conditions;
compare methods by cost per iteration and robustness;
prepare the transition to reduced PDE-constrained optimization.

Most unconstrained methods fit, at a high level, the same iterative template:

if condition fails, the step is too optimistic for local curvature;
backtracking is cheap and usually enough in practice.

children.value: storage) and often faster than GD;
practical performance depends strongly on line-search quality and resets.

(the four authors who
proposed this update family).

Core idea: mimic Newton’s method without computing the exact Hessian.
Instead, build a sequence of positive-definite inverse-Hessian approximations
from gradient differences, so directions keep curvature information while each
iteration only needs function/gradient evaluations.

children.html: Hk≈∇2f(uk)−1H_k\approx \nabla^2 f(u_k)^{-1}Hk​≈∇2f(uk​)−1
pk=−Hkgk.p_k=-H_k g_k.pk​=−Hk​gk​.
sk=uk+1−uk,yk=gk+1−gk,ykTsk>0,s_k=u_{k+1}-u_k,\qquad y_k=g_{k+1}-g_k,\qquad y_k^T s_k>0,sk​=uk+1​−uk​,yk​=gk+1​−gk​,ykT​sk​>0,

H_{k+1}=\left(I-\rho_k s_k y_k^T\right)H_k\left(I-\rho_k y_k s_k^T\right)+\rho_k s_k s_k^T,
\quad \rho_k=\frac{1}{y_k^T s_k}.

children.value:
So the inverse-Hessian approximation is updated through rank-1 outer-product
terms (overall a rank-2 correction per iteration).
much faster than GD on ill-conditioned smooth problems;
good local behavior (often superlinear with standard assumptions);
more memory and linear algebra than GD/CG.

For large scale: use L-BFGS (limited-memory variant):
keep only the last

children.value: very robust under nonconvexity or poor Hessian quality;
Typical subproblem solvers: Cauchy step, dogleg, truncated CG.
: safest baseline, cheapest iteration, slowest asymptotically.
: cheap memory, often better than GD, more tuning-sensitive.
: strong default for smooth medium-size problems.
iteration count matters less than “PDE solves per accepted step”;
GD + Armijo on an anisotropic quadratic.
Nonlinear CG (FR and PR+) on Rosenbrock.
BFGS vs GD comparison on Rosenbrock.
Trust-region (dogleg) on a shifted nonconvex quadratic.

example that reuses code meant to be shared by later lectures.
